content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

November 1, 1995

Mary Lindley
Assistant to the City Manager
City of Moorpark
799 Moorpark Avenue
Moorpark, CA 93021

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-339

Dear Ms. Lindley:

This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
City of Moorpark Mayor Paul W. Lawrason, Jr., and City
Councilmembers Bernardo M. Perez, Patrick Hunter, Scott
Montgomery, and John E. Wozniak under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act.¹ This letter will also
serve to confirm previously issued telephone advice.

QUESTION

Will the City Council's retirement contributions in the
California Public Employees' Retirement System (Cal-PERS)
inhibit their ability to negotiate a solid waste contract with an
entity in which Cal-PERS invests its funds?

CONCLUSION

No. Funds withheld pursuant to the California Public
Employees' Retirement System are neither "investments" nor
"income" under the Act. The City Councilmembers are not
prohibited from negotiating a new contract for solid waste
disposal.

___________________________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear in 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. A-95-339
Page 2


FACTS

The City of Moorpark will be negotiating a new solid waste
contract for the collection and disposal of refuse with two of its
existing contractors. One of the companies involved in
negotiation is currently under bankruptcy. The bankruptcy trustee
is considering an offer to purchase the refuse company from Aroura
Investment Capital. Cal-PERS invests in Aroura Investment
Capital.

The Mayor and City Councilmembers participate in Cal-PERS and
are concerned that this could impede their ability to participate
in these negotiations.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in the making, or using their official position to
influence a governmental decision in which they know or have
reason to know they have a financial interest.

A public official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate
family, or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

* * *

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(Sections 87103(a) and (c).)

The term "investment" as used above means, in pertinent part,
any financial interest in or security issued by a business entity,
including but not limited to common stock, preferred stock,
rights, warrants, options, debt instruments and any partnership or
other ownership interest owned directly, indirectly or
beneficially by the public official, or his or her immediate
family. The term "investment" does not include a time or demand
deposit in a financial institution, shares in a credit union, any

Advice File No. A-95-339
Page 3


insurance policy, interest in a diversified mutual fund registered
with the Securities and Exchange Commission under the Investment
Company Act of 1940 or a common trust fund which is created
pursuant to Section 1564 of the Financial Code. (Section 82034.)

The term "income" as used above, in pertinent part, a
payment received, including but not limited to any salary, wage,
advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift,
loan, etc. The term "income" does not include payments received
under a defined benefit pension plan qualified under Internal
Revenue Code Section 401(a). (Section 82030.)

The Commission has previously determined that neither an
interest in a private profit sharing plan, nor the investments
held through it, are "investments" within the meaning of the Act,
if the plan is a defined benefit pension plan under Internal
Revenue Code Section 401(a). (Harris Advice Letter No. A-82-207.)
This advice is consistent with the Act's provision exempting
documents under a defined benefit pension plan from the definition
of "income." Additionally, the Commission has held that sums
withheld pursuant to the Public Employees Retirement System are
not "investments" and the earnings of such sums are not "income."
(In re Elmore (1977) 4 FPPC Ops. 8.)

Accordingly, absent some other disqualifying economic
interest as set forth in Section 87103, Moorpark Mayor Paul W.
Lawrason, Jr., and City Councilmembers Bernardo N. Perez, Patrick
Hunter, Scott Montgomery, and John E. Wozniak do not have an
economic interest in Cal-PERS by virtue of the retirement
contributions held by Cal-PERS. Therefore, they are not
prohibited from participating in the negotiation process relating
to the award of a contract for solid waste disposal.

I trust this answers your question sufficiently. If you need
anything further, or wish to discuss this, please feel free to
contact the Commission's Legal Division at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

SGC/JET/jt
